economical with the sound. So, you know, you could type this or you could type softer. Type softer. So you're live? Live? How do you close the lid? Oh, hi here. Sorry. Just... Let's officially start. Let's roll. How's everybody doing this week? Hope you all had a great week. I got amazing feedback on last week's train. And this week, these are my favorite topics. And what we're going to be going through is, you know, you could refer to it as prospecting or qualifying, but it's the most misunderstood of all aspects of sales and influence. And not just in the traditional sales, I think in any setting, when you're just simply trying to interact with another human being to be a social setting, doesn't matter what it is, you even talk to your children is essentially how do you go about getting your point across and influencing someone? In other words, we call the straight line. I refer to it as goal oriented communication, meaning it's communication where you have a specific end in mind. You're trying to accomplish something, right? That's what the straight line is. So it's not just for selling in traditional sense, right? It's for anything with you trying to get a certain point of course, right? And with the straight license that we are able to divide that up into steps, into discernible steps that are repeatable, that are easy to learn, right? And at the end of the day, we're going to get you your outcome. They're going to allow you to get your point across in a way that moves the other person both logically and emotionally, and that cause them to ultimately say yes to buy, right? So this particular step is the most misunderstood one of all for a number of different reasons. And it's also a step that's typically short and abbreviated, blunted by most salespeople because they get nervous about it. And they feel that if they continue on with this step, that they're going to actually break rapport. And in fact, they're actually building rapport. So it's so misunderstood. So we're going to get started, let me formally in about one minute. I'm just going to very quickly tell you about today's training and next week. So that's that. Right. And now let's move on to the actual training. Here we go. The word that basically best describes this whole process is something called prospect. That's the term that we use in traditional sales. I mean, some people call it qualifying and I use this term myself, you know, occasionally when I'm talking about prospect, the big mistake that, and the concern I have when people talk about qualifying is they tend to think of it in very narrow terms. Like when you say I'm in the qualifying session and then you need a qualifying end to see if they can afford your product, right? And to make sure that they need your product. They basically, that's like, that's the main thing, is it that they financially qualify and do they want what I'm selling. And those are the two basic elements that most people focus on when they qualify, right? But when we talk about qualifying, which we really call intelligence gathering with straight line, it's a whole different thing. It's a completely different animal. So, whatever possible, I want you to think of this process as intelligence gathering and not just simply qualifying. When you think about it in terms of qualifying, you tend to focus on a couple of things and you miss the boat on the whole thing. Because here's the bottom line. I'm going to tell you this flat out, all right? The secret to my success in the front half of a sale, meaning before I asked for the order for the first time. Let me draw it out for you, watch. This is your straight line, right? This is your opening with a sale. But again, that's your close, right? And then all boundaries above the full line. So, I'm going to conclude out, right? You're going to stay in between these two boundaries, the in control of the sale, the as out of control. So, the goal is we want to stay in control. Keep between these boundaries. And I'll explain exactly what that means. Because this is where it really starts to get important, while you're prospecting and while you're gathering intelligence. So, imagine this is the opening, the first second that you open up your mouth to speak. Whether it's over the phone, maybe you're going door to door, you walk in someone's office, you own a business with people come in to you. It's that first moment of contact where you either open up your mouth, the prospect lays eyes on you. It could be even over the internet. Let me just tell you the bottom line here. The internet, I'm not going to say the internet design. Funnels that I use to bring in potential clients, right? That's my online marketing, right? So, marketing is what happens essentially to bring in people to your funnel. And then we settle them, we close them to monetize our marketing, to make money, you have to close them. Now, the biggest mistake that internet marketers make, people that self-stop online, is they think that if they perfect internet marketing, they just get so great at it. And their copy is so tight and right. And I teach copywriting as well, right? They think they get it so great. And their funnel is so awesome, they'll never have to speak to a human being. They can just hide behind their computer and reap their awards of being an online model. And it's just fucking bullshit, guys. It's not true at a certain point in time. If you want to make real money, you're going to have to speak to another human being. Now, forget about all the business, the non-failure, but business aspects of trying to deal with your employees, your credit card processor, all the other things that go into building a business. So besides the sales aspect, even in the sales aspect, you can't just be online because there's a certain cap to how much you can get from solar. It really is about $2,000, maybe a little bit more, $2,000, $4,000 tops, right? But if you want to really get big money from solar, and I mean that in a positive way, you're just going to have major value and you want to get a $20,000 sale, $50,000 sale, or bigger than that, you're not going to do without having a conversation with them. So at a certain point in time to take things to the next level, you're going to have to either get in the phone, see someone in person. It's going to come down to a conversation. And that's when the straight line really kicks in. It covers everything before that and says that the rules that we have to straight line selling are also the straight line market. They're very similar to what you do to attract people as well. But just remember this, to really make money in this world, to really build a business, you're going to have to get proficient in communication. Bottom line. So watch, going back to the straight line, this is that moment where it just starts, I don't know how it starts, right? Off-line, online, in person, over the phone, door to door, right? You make your first few seconds, you have to make that all-important first impression. Sharpest attack, enthusiastic sale, expert in your field, right? That's your first impression. You're going to make that straight away because without that, what happens? Well, you're perceived as being a duller, not an expert in your field, you're not enthusiastic. And people say, you know what? It's not a person worth listening to, and they tune out. Now, that's one aspect. The other aspect is not only do they say, you know what? It's not really a person worth listening to, but they also say, fuck it, I'm going to be in control because I'm the dominant person on this conversation. I'm the expert. I know more than this schnook. So they automatically try to take control of the conversation. So what happens if you don't establish yourself as an expert in the beginning, you'll spend a lot of time and control of the sale where the prospect is told and there are all sorts of stuff that's not your main to the conversation, and I see this happen again and again and again with untrained sales. Okay? So again, we take control, right? And then we get to the point here, because it says right about over here, right? Where we start to gather intelligence. We start to ask our prospect questions to see if he financially qualifies for a product, if he truly wants our product, if he needs our product, if he can benefit from our product, what his beliefs are about buying, about salespeople in general, what his values are, his hierarchy of values, what is his most important values and ultimately the most important one of all is where his pain lies. What's that one worry, whether it's financial or any type of worry that keeps him up at night or her up at night and sits in the back of his skull and weighs them down like an anchor basically, makes their life just feel heavier and more oppressive. And at the end of the day, the key to really closing, especially the tougher prospects, is you want to resolve someone's pain. And the way we resolve that pain is by offering our solution, which comes next. So we have our first four seconds, right? And then we transition from that into this prospecting phase. We start gathering intelligence. We start gathering intelligence. Now, when I say gather intelligence, what I'm talking about is you want to find out everything that we might possibly need to know to close the deal. Anything that's germane to the sales process to close the deal. And here's now where I'm getting back to the initial thing I said. If there's one secret to the front half of what I do, the front half, is what happens up to here when I ask for the order for the first time. So this has been reality on the terrible walks, I know, right? So let me just redraw this here, right? So reality is how we look. This is the straight line and this is the open and this is the close here. The truth is that the front half of the sales is more like a third. It's not over here. It's not usually only a third of the way down one. That varies based on your industry and also how many calls are in your sales cycle. But the bottom line is you're not typically halfway done when you ask for the order for the first time. And I want you to really be clear on that because emotionally, you can't be like, I just did this great presentation and it's such a great job. I know he's feeling really good about my product and he trusts me. I'm going to ask for the order and it's a good job he's going to buy. And then when the prospect says, oh, it sounds good, let me think. But you're like, oh, and you're deflated. That's a problem. So you need to emotionally set yourself up for the first objection, the first stall. That's crucial. So then when you get that stall slash objection, right? Then we begin the back half of the sale and the back half of the sale consists of loopholes, which I went through if you could stop. Okay. And I'll go through it again. We're just hitting things through different items. So remember, front half of the sale, right? Starts from the first four seconds and runs to the point where you give your full presentation and you ask for the order. From the first place, ask for the order. That's front half. Back half or back two thirds starts from when you get hit with the first objection and then you go through the looping process. Now, as I say, in the back half of the sale, the secret sauce to the back half is looping, meaning how do you take an objection and use it as an opportunity to build more certainty for Egypt, the three tenths, and then seamlessly transition into a close without breaking the report. And that caveat, without breaking the report is the most important part, because that's what most will do in the back half when they start getting into the objection handling phase. They start getting into some attestive wills or argument with the client, because what you're really saying when you overcome an objection is, you're wrong, I'm right. Your way of thinking is wrong, mine is right. And that sets you up to break the report. So we don't do that with a straight line, right? So that's the back half. Now, in the front half, right? So what does the front half consist of? The first four seconds, right? It consists of your intelligence gathering phase, three, rapport building, right? And then your transition is level four. I need you to get my right to the question. Level five is your present presentation. And number six would be you asked for the order. That's the front half, okay? From here to here. The secret source to the front half is this. Steps two and three. Intelligence gathering and rapport building. And that's what I really naturally do really, really well. And I want to teach you to do it the same way. It's very easy to do. It's duplicatable. I've taught millions and millions of people how to become expert prospectors. And the term that we use for this is prospecting, okay? And that goes back to like that, you know, the visual of the old scraggly bearded prospector who's prospecting for gold with a tin pan. He stands over the slide of the stream of this tin pan and he sifts through water. He sifts through more water and more water. You know, millions of gallons of water flow through his pan, right? And he's just searching for that one nugget of gold to make it all worth it. Now, remember, he's not trying to turn the water into gold. He's sifting out through the water to find gold. And that's what we do. We don't turn non-buyers into buyers. We try to sift through all the people that come into our sales funnel and find the ones who are potential buyers and make presentation to those people. And I'll repeat that again because it might rip, Leo. In other words, when we prospect what we're doing, okay? I'm going to say this very slowly. This is the biggest source of time wasting for pretty much not just new sales or all salespeople. When you prospect what you're doing is you're sifting through all the buyers. All the potential buyers of gold. All the potential buyers who enter your sales funnel and you're extracting out the ones who might potentially buy from you, who qualify. That's why be careful with that word. They qualify who might potentially buy and the ones who are not dangerous, not qualified. They're not going to buy. They can afford it. They don't want your product really. They don't need your product. Maybe they just dragged it to the funnel by accident. Out of the sales funnel as quickly and elegantly as possible. I'll repeat that. You want to eliminate the non-buyers out of your sales funnel as quickly and elegantly as possible. Now, why is that? Very simply is that the chief mistake that rookie sales people make and even experienced ones to is they get to this point when they're selling and they get a little bit desperate for leads and they start cherishing each individual lead and the operative word there is lead. Not if it's a lead. It's not making a buyer. They value each lead so greatly, they don't want to discard it. Rather than say, you know what? This person is never going to buy from me and getting in that funnel. They will actually make a full blown presentation, finish cross maybe by some slim imagination that was wrong and this person will buy and God bless if he does or she does. That is the source of so much destruction for a sales person because what happens is two things. One, not only is it obviously a massive waste of time. Just to stick to this mathematically, right? If a hundred people enter your sales funnel and it takes you 15 minutes to go through a full blown presentation, right, start to finish on average and you decide you don't care what the fuck, I'm going to make sure that all hundred of these here in my presentation will guess what, you're not going to give a hundred 15 minute presentations, half of each have no shot of turning into a sale but you spent all that time, 50 people times 50 minutes, 750 fucking minutes. It's 12 hours, okay, 12 or 13 hours, okay, 12 and a half hours, right, a lot of fucking time you spent, okay. While you could have taken those people and then you spent three minutes going through your qualifying, eliminating them from the pipeline, save yourself 10 hours and that over a year my friend, it's just the difference between success and failure and here's even worse. So again, obviously time wise, it's the biggest fucking waste of time in the world to talk to people who are never going to buy from you. Really, it's just a joke, right? Yeah, people do it all the time, right? The second part and even more destructive believe it or not is the fact that it ends up creating limiting beliefs inside of you because you're getting bad intelligence because you don't say, you say, fuck, I'm talking to all these people and they just like buying from me. I wonder what's wrong with me. Maybe I suck at it, am I not a good salesman? Is my pitch off? Maybe I'm not closing right? Maybe I'm doing something wrong here and then you start changing shit or maybe you give up entirely and you know what? You were doing it all based on bad intelligence and you sabotage yourself and you end up thinking you know what, maybe I'm just not cut out for sales. I couldn't have closed them. No one could have closed them. You can't close people who are encloseable. One thing I always say about the straight line, you hear me say many times, when you get to the level where you're proficient, not even that's just proficient, you get to this point where you can close anyone who's closeable and I promise you that's true, promise you that's true, but that doesn't mean you close everybody because there are some people who simply are encloseable and then there are other people who simply shouldn't be closed for ethical reasons or whatever. You just can't help them, right? So that being said, if there are some people who are encloseable, if you're spending your time speaking to them, you think, God, I'm just banging my head against the wall. What the fuck? It's the biggest waste of time. In the brokerage business we call it getting wooden leads. You're giving me wood in my pipeline because it looks like a lead, it smells like a lead, but it ain't a lead. It's not a real lead. So this whole process comes down to prospecting, intelligence gathering. The basis of prospecting, here's what it is, right? You're sifting through all the leads, all the people, the human beings that the marketing department handed you. In other words, you have marketing on one side of the equation and you have sales on the other, right? Marketing is the process by which you sift through the potential universe of buyers, whether it's online marketing, off TV commercials, telemark, doesn't door to door. You're going through all these, the gross number of people and then you're netting out the people who say, yes, I might be interested. They respond to an ad. They say, yeah, I'd like to hear about your idea. They walk in your door, they click on your, so they opt in, they give you the, those are the people that come from your marketing efforts. Now that could be from a marketing department in a bigger company or you could be as a sales person doing your own marketing, which happens very often in small companies where the sales person ends up being the marketer and the sales person. Now, when I consult for companies, I do everything possible to have management take back control of marketing because I believe it makes the salesman infinitely more effective. If I'm in a company, I would, I would do the marketing for my sales. I want to hand my salespeople, qualify these and let them only be closing because I believe the highest value of a sales person should be selling and they might suck at marketing. I just went through this with a company that I could sell for in the logistics industry. And I went through the whole straight line. I, I, I, I, I, I, I, I was a product line of mine and we had amazing phenomenal results, phenomenal. Right? But what they noticed was that the end of the day, they couldn't get it to go to the highest level because no one could make enough dials. They were running out of leads. So I went back and said, listen, I told you this was gonna happen because once I had them actually going out there and dialing the right way, they could go through a lot of potential people. This company had his sales force getting their own leads. So they were spending 75% of their day trying to find people to call. And then because of that, they valued each lead so much. So I had the manager as a owner, as a test, as just trust me, tested with five or six people. And I think give them, buy the leads yourself and hand them a stack of 100 leads per day, a 75 per day. Okay, they expect to make 250 calls. How can they do that with 75? Easy, because they build up. Over a few days, you'll have a stack of leads, right? And guess what? His average calls went from 25 calls per day per salesman to 250, 10 times. So the impact as a business owner of taking back control of your marketing and just giving your sales, and looking at it this way, you're giving them the fuel to put inside the automobile so the automobile could run. Without leads, salespeople become very ineffective. You get it? So again, marketing brings in the leads and then the sales force is there, or you, or sales president, is there to slam them close, to close it, to monetize it, right? Does that make sense? Pretty basic stuff, okay? And it makes sense to you, I want you to go into your thing and press the number of calls you on the Patreon, press yes, I know everyone gets it. I'll give you a couple of seconds to do that. Everyone responds and makes sense, right? This is an integral part of selling. We're getting responses, Bo? Good. Is there anyone that's in the 15 minute walk here or something like that? Huh, good. Okay, 30 minutes, okay, great. What's that? It's almost 30 minutes, okay. I can fucking talk, right? Okay. You love it. All right, all right. Anyway, you guys enjoying this is a good, I'm telling you this is the best topic. But I was like, can this, they're having a conversation about how it's gonna apply to opposite sex and attracting. Of course! Listen, you know what Strayer said that? Because in the process of developing a straight line progress just for that, attracting the opposite, because there's no better program in reality. It's just not about getting late. It's about having someone fall in love with you, seriously. And you know, I get it. When I was young, I liked wanting to get late all the fucking time, right, Bo? That's what Bo would say. We had my son, right? The point is now I'm in love, you know? And I'm an older guy, you know? It takes a little bit more of a viagra. He says, I'm just kidding, okay? Anyway, Seattle's better, all right? But the point is, is that, what was I saying? Okay, the point is that it's not just about getting late. It's also about, you know, about how about having fun with someone, getting someone to really like you as a human being. You know, inside each of us is an infinitely likable person, seriously. And it's very difficult, I know from my parents, it's difficult to get that point across to someone else. You know, what's my value as a human being, not just money or looks or how good I am in bed or not in bed? What about me as a friend, as a person? And let me tell you something, there's no better way than to get that point across than through using the straight line system. And by the way, the key's gonna be prospecting, okay? Not only is the idea the more women or men you talk to, the more you're going to meet, but also the biggest mistake that people make is what? They know almost the cliche of getting, all the guy fucking did for 45 minutes was talk about himself. He don't give a fuck about, anyone gonna get, you know what? He didn't ask questions. Tell me about yourself. That's what prospecting is about. We talk about intelligence, that's what it is. You get it? It's about asking questions about, and also asking them in a certain way so you're not perceived as the grand inquisitor. Like, so tell me, you know, how much money are in the bank right now? You know, how many times do you get laid this week? You know, you know what I'm saying? What do you do for a living? You know, how much money? Well, you can see your balance sheet. You know, whatever it is you're doing, that's not, it's like, so tell me, you know, how long you've been out there in the market for? It's a certain tonality that we use with each question. Okay? And based on the tonality, you'll get either the right response and you will build rapport, or you'll get the wrong response and break rapport. Let me get very specific. I'll bring this back to Sy, I wanna, it's just such an important point, okay? So where we are is gonna quickly, explain the four buying archetypes. Let's say this archetypes is the word, A-R archetypes, right? The four buying archetypes are the people who enter your funnel from your marketing efforts, from your marketing department, I'm gonna fold the four categories. There's four categories. You can have what's called your buyers in heat. And these are your best buyers. These are people who need your product, want your product, can afford your product, and also feeling some sort of pain or uncomfortability. And what the fuck is that? Because it's an earthquake, okay? Pain or uncomfortability, and they want it now. There's an itch they gotta scratch. They're feeling some uncomfortability, so they want to buy now. They're hot to make their decision. That's your best buyer, and it's called buyer in heat. The only problem with that is that not that many of those, because the next category is called buyers in power. Now, buyer in power is the same exact thing as a buyer in heat, but the urgency is missing. They need your product, they want your product, they can afford your product, and then they'll buy a product like yours at some point. They're just not hot to buy it right now. There's no urgency, not feeling any pain, not saying, I gotta make this decision right now so my life is out of control in some way, or feeling some uncomfortability. So they feel like they're in the power, like I'll make the decision when I'm good and ready. Obviously, this buyer is easy to close with that buyer. These are both great buyers. Now, with a funnel, you might get 15 of these and 35 of those, and 100. Those are just the great difficulties on the internet. That's an approximation. That's what a percentage, right? Now, you have the other two categories called your looky-loose, your looky-loose, or your tire kickers, or your motor, or your tire kickers. I can't talk to you, right? These are people who disguise themselves as buyers in power, meaning they pretend that they're going to buy with their own power, but they have no fucking intention of buying their window shoppers, tire kickers. These are the ultimate time wasters. They pretend to be buyers in power, but they actually have no intention of buying. They just act like it. They're the most destructive people in your pipeline on these, and these are the ones that I was really talking about. When I talked about that wasting 12 hours, it's on these people who disguise themselves as buyers in power. The main difference between the two is typically financial qualification. Meaning these people just simply don't have the money, but they like to fuck in window shopper, talk about it, or they're just fricking delusional, okay? That's your third group. And you might have to say, but what are you those? Okay, there's a lot of them in there. And then the last group are called the people who are the mistakes, I call them, or the people who were dragged into your pipeline. Those are people who basically clicked on a site, opt in, they didn't really mean to, or they thought it was something else. They walked in the store, it's not even the right place. And wherever they are in a seminar room, they might have been friends, just dragged them in, come, you got us. So, but they're just not in the right place. They never had any intention of buying. They don't want to be there. Those are the ones that sit unsubscribed when you send them a mail. You get it? Those are your mistakes. So, there's about maybe 10 of those, okay? Four, right? Those are your 100 buyers. So, what you're doing here, as you take someone down the straight line from the open to the close, you're essentially sifting through these 100 people and extracting out these 50. The buyers in heat and buyers in power, and you're gonna continue moving them down the straight line by making full blown presentations to them. These people are, no, you're gonna actually eliminate them out of the pipeline as quickly and elegantly as possible, as I said. What am I elegantly? And I'm saying you're fucking wasting my time. Look at it, I'm at my store. I don't mean that, right? What I'm saying is in a certain place, you know, based on what you said to me, it's probably not gonna fit for you, you know, and maybe you could go somewhere else, but have a great day. Just end the call of the encounter as quickly as possible, but don't, you know, be mean about it, or piss someone off. Okay, it's just not good for business, right? But that's the goal. Is these people, so let's go back to what we've been talking about, to go over here. Now, do it. Check each other's minds. Yeah. Okay, so watch. Here's our straight line. Open, close, right? Our boundaries are moving below. Our first four seconds here, and then we just start asking questions, right? This is our intelligence gathering right here, okay? Now, when we gather intelligence, there's a second thing that's going on here, and it's the difference between success and failure. You ready? Here's the secret. As you're gathering intelligence, not only are you finding out whether or not they're qualified financially, whether they need your profit, they want your profit, all the things are pain, right? Not only are you doing that, but you're doing it in a way, and this is the golden ticket right here. You ready? Don't ever forget this. You're doing it in a way that allows you to build massive rapport on both a conscious and an unconscious level, and this, my friend, is the secret to the whole thing. When you gather intelligence, the purpose is not only to get the information that you need to decide whether or not it makes sense to make a full-blown presentation, right? So, in other words, what the first aim of it is to say, I know it's gonna be about half of the, and by the way, what dictates what percentage of your pipeline's gonna be buyers and power and buyers of heat versus the look you lose in mistakes? Is it 50, 50? Is it 60? What's gonna dictate that? Answer? How laser-guided, how targeted your marketing was. The better the marketing, the more laser-guided it was, the more appropriate people were targeted, the better off, the higher those percentage. So, in a really kick-ass marketing campaign, 100, if you might get 75 people that are potentially buyers, they're in their first case, and only in a really poor campaign when they just get everybody, it could be 30, 70, the other way. So, just remember, that's not a static number, it depends on the quality of the marketing. Makes sense? It does, logical, right? Now, when you gather intelligence, okay, as you're asking these questions, not only are you identifying all things you know to close the sale, to close the sale, right? But you're doing it in a way that gets you into this ultra-type report. How do you do that? How do you step by step? That's really what straight-line prospecting is about. And you end up, and you save the time, the agony, and also the limiting beliefs that come from pitching your heart and soul out, and not closing. What am I doing wrong? Nothing, no one could have closed it. Okay, so. Last tip before we go through the 10 things, this is crucial. What you're doing when you gather intelligence, is you're also identifying pain. And the rest of the last thing you do is you wanna identify where they're pain-loans. What's that worry, that deep worry that sits at the base and that's going to always end up, right? Well, the reason why you're doing that is because that is gonna be how you take a buyer in power and turn them into a buyer in heat. A buyer in power is not gonna buy from you. You can make a great presentation to them, they'll say, wait, it sounds great. Let me think about it. That's what buyers in power do. They say, I'll make a decision when I'm good and ready. Not gonna make it now. That's what buyers in power do. And let me just tell you something. At the end of the day, if you and I went side by side, okay, I promise you, I will have closed any salesman in the world, in any product, okay? The difference is not gonna be the buyers in heat. In other words, you'll close the buyers in heat and sell the line. Those are the easy ones. Anyone in monkey clothes will, they have a problem they're feeling pain. They're gonna buy from anybody who just has a mix of time to go for the pitch. The difference is I am gonna also close pretty much every single buyer in power. Well, an untrained salesman will not. And that's why the closing rate from a professional straight line pro is so much higher because a straight line pro knows how to take a buyer in power and turn them into a buyer in heat. That's why do I take someone who was star roll for the buyer in power, they need in my product, they want a product, they could use that product. They have no pain though, they're not worried about something. And well, maybe they haven't worried but they buried during denial of that pain. And during this intelligence gathering phase, I uncover, I figure out, I find out where that pain is and I'll amplify that pain a little bit. Won't solve the pain yet. I'll say, oh, I can solve, I don't do it. I keep them in that amplified state and then I make my presentation while they're feeling that pain. And then at the very end, I reintroduce that pain and amplify it even more. And that's what flips a buyer in power to buy a heat and bam, that's how I close the tougher buyers also with lowering action threshold. And my looping is over the key is pain, uncomfortability. What separates these two buyers, a heat and power, is buyers in power do not feel pain whether they're denial or they're pain. Buyers in heat and heart broke through the surface and they gotta act down. So the reason why it's so destructive to just say, yeah, I'm qualifying to find out if they need my product and they can afford product, you never found out where their pain was. What's that deepness for, what's that values? What are the beliefs you can use that then to amplify pain and close the sales. And that's why an average salesman has X clothes of great salesmen clothes is much higher because they get the buyers in power as well. Bottom line, okay? So that's the sort of, you know, the sort of the technical informational side of what we do as prospectors, right? In the straight line. Let's go through our 10 steps here right now. Number one, first step. I already mentioned this building, I'm gonna reinforce it. You are a sister, not an alchemist. In other words, an alchemist is, you know, one of those people from the olden days back in the 15, 60, they used to try to turn lead into gold, turn a base metal, turn something into something else. That's not what we do. We don't turn non buyers into buyers, to fool's errand. And it's what I've been talking about for the whole time. We are sifters, we sift through 100 people to find out the 50 potentially great ones and close the ones that we can. That's what you do. You're a sifter, not alchemist. You don't turn one thing into another. Number two, always use a straight. In other words, I care whether you're selling in person or over the phone. You always have a script in your own mind. You don't have to be reading from it. But you wanna, this is going to be the best thing to say in every situation, in every aspect of the sale, there's only gonna be the best way to say it. Now, before I go through this, I wanna dig into this. Let me give you the next one, number three. Which is you wanna ask permission to ask questions. You don't just start interrogating someone. You don't just say, oh, let me, and just to say, so, how much do you have in the market? You don't do that. The first thing is you let me just ask you a couple quick questions so I can best serve you. Couple of questions so I don't waste your time. Let me ask you a couple of questions so I can listen to exactly what you need. Ask for permission and that word so, I wanna ask you a couple of questions so I don't waste your time. Always use that justifier, so I don't waste time, so I can best serve you so I can see exactly what you need that's so dramatically improves your outcome. So you wanna always use your justifier, okay? Step, now I'm gonna go with that. Let me go back to the script. When I told them not to have the script, I mean, there's gonna be a best way to ask for permission. And most importantly, it's gonna be a best set of questions to ask. You're gonna have certain questions that you need to ask based on your industry. Now there are some generic questions, right, that apply to every industry, but for the most part, there's gonna be a specialized set of questions that you have to ask someone in order to essentially find out all things you need to know. There's two ways to do that. One, you can fucking wing it. Don't write everything down, don't plan it out, just try to fucking say, oh, remember them all in the right order. That's ridiculous. Strategic preparation, meaning you wanna prepare yourself for each sales encounter. Write down exactly what's the best way in your industry to ask for permission to ask questions. Write down all the best questions and exactly the right order. What do I mean by that? Okay, that goes to the next watch. You wanna go always from big picture questions to small picture questions. You don't stop off with the minutiae. You go from big picture questions to small picture questions. And even more clearly than that, you go from less invasive, this is one of the keys to the key, less invasive to more invasive. And this is one I wanna slow down when I really get into. One of the biggest mistakes that people make when they qualify is they start off asking invasive questions. Like, so tell me, how much money do you have in the bank right now? You know, how much do you earn each year? I'm saying, what the fuck you? I just met you. If you start off with an invasive question that cuts into what someone's financial qualification is or if they're sick or whatever your industry is, you know, it doesn't matter. It points whatever those questions are invasive, you wanna save those for later on. Start off with your noninvasive questions. In questions that any reasonable person would ask. So tell me, how long have you been living in the area for? Not what's your home address? How much is your house worth? No, oh, so as you know, it's a beautiful home. Tell me how long you're living here for, where'd you move from? So what happens is by going from less invasive questions to more invasive questions, when you ask that invasive question, so tell me, how much you have in the bank right now? They'll answer you. If it's the third or fourth question, and you've sort of, you know, peeled back the layers of the onion, so to speak, right? People will answer very invasive questions in person and over the phone, but you have to start off with noninvasive questions first. If you start off trying to pry too deep, people do break rapport and the sale ends before it starts, okay? That's next. Number one, this is now for the six. This is a huge one, right? This is also the key now to the second aspect of prospecting, which is building rapport. You need to be using the correct tonality. Each question has its own best tonality, and this is the secret, this is the keys to the kingdom. In other words, if you ask the question one way, you'll break rapport and get a negative response. Say to someone, so tell me, you know, what's your biggest worry right now? You know, let's say your shirt is open, so what's gonna happen right now, if you die right now, how's your family gonna suffer from that, huh? Like, fuck you. Boy, you can say, you also tell me, what's the worst that could happen right now? You have got to get your pass away, so they have, how would your family be impacted? See, if I care, I feel your pain. I'm just like you. Tell me, empathy, sympathy, that tonality is crucial. And let me just tell you, each individual question is going to have its own best tonality. So that's why also you need to basically look, that's why I say you wanna strip out all your questions first, and then say, okay, now what's my best tonality for this one, what's my best tonality for that one, and go through them and say, what is my best tonality for each question, and cracks it, and make sure you ask tonality, the question with the right tonality. Watch what happens. If you ask the question with the wrong tonality here, so what's your deepest worry right now? What's really bothering you right now? I'm saying, fuck, you break for poor, and it's over. Or say, what's your deepest worry right now? What's really keeping you up at night? The guy says, wow, this salesperson really cares. They can't help. It's an unconscious response. Remember, tonality cuts through the unconscious and goes straight to the gut, to the unconscious, and it creates a gut reaction. This person cares about me. It's absolutely crucial. If you ask the wrong tonality, you're screwed. Right tonality is golden, okay? Next, okay? Here's the big one, there's all the big one. And this too, these two are the keys to the kingdom for building rapport. Use the correct body language as they respond. In other words, and also just people, especially as they respond. Let me explain. There's something I teach in the straight line system in various parts of it. It's called active listening. Active listening is the art of listening to someone else speak and giving them positive feedback, giving them energy back. So they say, wow, this person understands and they get me in the shop. So I ask you a question, right? And you respond, I'm like, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Yep, got it, uh-huh, uh-huh. I give you the a-hums, the yups, got yup. I give you that assurance, I get you, I understand you were on the same page. And let me just tell you something, there is nothing more powerful for building rapport than to ask someone a question using the right tonality and then say, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Yeah, oh yeah, got it, yep, makes a total set, yep, got it, yep. Now, there's an actual video of me out there doing a sales, a really funny story. And I will tell you this story next time, it's a long story, but you can actually see this after and I'll put the links to this thing at the end. Okay guys, and remember you posted your questions on the Patreon link, and this first question is a two bar question, and he says, you say some people spend an hour closing someone, when it can be done in five minutes, what do they do wrong? And is it true that there's virtually no difference in closing a $2,000 account, and a $30,000 account? Thanks. Well, the same there's no difference. Okay, so you're using numbers of 2,000, 30,000 without qualifying what, you know, $2,000 to one person is an awful lot of money, and $30,000 to another person is nothing. Like my friend James, you know, he spends $30,000, he sneezes and $30,000 comes out of his bucket, is on the floor and doesn't care, walk away, there's a billionaire, right? Other people, $2,000 is their life fucking savings. So you can't say like, let's try to reframe the question here. You know, is there a difference between selling big ticket items and small ticket items? The answer is yes and no. The same core elements must line up, whether it's a $500 product or a $5,000 product. They must, you know, the three tens, right? They have to love the product logically, emotionally, trust and connect with you, trust and connect with the company that stands behind the product, then you have to lower their action threshold with the necessary and add on paying it there and denial of the pay. Those, so it's always the same that those things have to happen. With the bigger ticket sales, sometimes there's more things involved as well, like for instance, there's multiple decision makers involved and also it's not just money, it's not just the money that dictates how carefully someone needs to make a decision, it's what is the impact when the person has to implement that decision? Someone buying something for themselves when it's gonna be a luxury item is gonna be very different than someone investing $30,000 in a new piece of infrastructure that everyone's gonna have to adopt in their company and everyone's gonna have to use and people will sign off on it. If it doesn't work, it's a fucking disaster. So it's not like that. You know, you're focusing on money as being the end goal of what makes one sale, one money. It's not. It has to do with you could be selling a $500 product but that product is gonna have even more implications than a $50,000 purchase. That's something we can make and it's the implications of what has to happen in terms of what does it impact and the company could goes wrong that creates the tougher sales. The shortest distance in any two points is a straight line. This is zero open, this is your close. So obviously, if you're gonna go like this, like, you know, to get here, it's gonna take a lot longer than a straight line when you open to the close. So I don't wanna say it takes a certain amount of time to open to any sale, but the goal with the straight line is to keep it within these healthy boundaries here and slowly but surely edging this sale down the straight line when you open to the close. Next question. How do you make qualifying questions? And at what stage do you start asking? Is there a limit to how many questions you should ask? The limit is you don't want to ask one more question than you have to, and don't ask one less question than you have to. Work backwards. It's a straight line, always works backwards. What things do you legitimately need to know? Like, for instance, when I'm, I have a mastermind program for very high end people. And by the way, if you'd like to join, send, send, up right, you know, you can see, by the way, yeah, if you want to join it, you know, you have to qualify for it. So we're very successful people, right? And it's, you know, 11 people sitting around, when I send out an email about that to my high end players, and I say, fill out this questionnaire, I write out all the questions, I go through the questions, I say, do I really need to ask this? Or am I just asking it for my own fucking health? Like, am I asking it because I just want to ask a question? Does it really give me legitimate intelligence I need to close? Don't ask questions just for the sake of asking questions. Ask what you need to ask and not one question more, but make sure that you ask an exhaustive line of questioning. There's going to be things you need to know. Find out what they are and work backwards. Okay? One more? Yeah, I'll do one more. Let's, I want to pick a game like that. I don't know, I'm trying to blow up. Okay, one more, one more, one more, one more, one more, sorry, sorry, sorry. Uh, can you, someone says, can you use tonality in writing letters? Of course, this isn't really prospecting, but of course, so this is one of the things that I had to learn myself when I wrote books, because you know, essentially writing, it's like tonality on paper. The way you do that in direct marketing, and you know, on the internet or in sales letters, right, is you want to italicize certain things, put things in caps, bold, underline, okay? Drop certain words, okay? Where, you know, what you're looking to do here is you're going to read your copy back to yourself and say, is this how I would really sound if I was sounding great? It has to read great. So of course, using my secrets, so I use very short sentences, very short paragraphs. I, I paralyze things, I underline them, and I make sure certain things are in like staccato-like beats, and I'll put things a little bit longer. So yes, of course you can. You know, it's too much to answer right now, but the answer is yes, and you know, I'll discuss that in some other training, okay?
